content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies featuring little girls going on adventures?

**Score:** 46
**Body:** I really enjoy movies that feature precocious (or average) girls that embark on their own adventures!  I guess it must be my inner child speaking out😂

I prefer live-action movies, but I do enjoy animated ones. I love my 80’s dark-fantasy style movies, however the style or era doesn’t matter to much to me! I don’t mind if the movie is scary or lighthearted, just try not to suggest anything to corny!

Some movies I like include Annie, Coraline, Matilda, Madeline, The Nutcracker and the Four Realms, Labyrinth, and Return to Oz. 

Thank you for anyone suggestions :)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Kiki’s Delivery Service

Spirited Away
  - To add to the Ghibli list:  
  
When Marnie Was Here  
My Neighbour Totoro  
The Cat Returns  
The Secret World of Arrietty
2. The Journey of Natty Gann, one of favorites as a kid and not talked about enough!
3. True Grit might fit…
4. Petite Maman

Moon Garden

Pan's Labyrinth (sad ending sorry)

The Fall
5. Ponyo

My Neighbor Totoro 

Paper Moon
6. Pippy Longstockings
7. The Golden Compass

Courage Mountain

Golddiggers: The Secret of Bear Mountain 

Not 100% on the corniness lol
8. These are more "young girls doing adventurous things" rather than going on long adventures, but I highly recommend them: 

Beasts of the Southern Wild

The Secret of Roan Inish

The Secret Garden (1993)
9. Spirited Away (2001)

## 2. Movies about someone who decides to say ‘Fuck it’ and live life for themselves

**Score:** 39
**Body:** Please recommend me a movie where someone gets tired of letting other people dictate how they live their own life, and instead decides to live life for themselves. Please recommend!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Office Space
2. Into the Wild (2007)
3. Captain Fantastic (2016) - not a singular character living for themself, but parents of six kids live completely off the grid and teach the children to be completely self-sufficient in the wild. But also school them in traditional subjects like math, literature, philosophy, etc. And the kids are all extremely advanced in those areas, too. The film starts right after the mom dies, and the dad has to take the family out of their rustic life and into the outside world for the first time. They meet with the deceased wife's family who disapproved of their lifestyle. 

It's a great movie with wonderful performances from Viggo Mortensen and all the kids.
4. The Big Lebowski
5. American Beauty
6. The Secret Life of Walter Mitty (2013) and Jerry Maguire (1996).
7. *Falling Down*
8. Falling Down?

The Truman Show, maybe.

Legally Blonde

Thelma and Louise

Fight Club

Shirley Valentine
9. If you want to be very literal, The Adjustment Bureau.
10. Crouching tiger hidden dragon 

Lost in America 

But I'm a cheerleader

## 3. The main character is getting fucked up by life

**Score:** 29
**Body:** Hi! I’m looking for movies where the main character is going through a really rough time and nothing seems to be going right - family, home, job or school, finances, health.. everything just keeps piling on them.

If you know movies like this, please share them with me. Thank you in advance!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Falling Down.
  - We stopped serving breakfast at eleven thirty, sir.
2. inside Llewelyn davis. it is 100% his fault but man what a great watch.
3. "The Pursuit of Happyness" (2006)
4. The big Lebowski
5. The Money Pit, about a house
6. A Serious Man (2009)
7. The Pianist
8. Dancer In The Dark
9. Hope Floats, at least for the first half of it. 

Home for the Holiday’s with Holly Hunter

Pieces of April

## 4. Looking for movies featuring absolute badasses (Mission: Impossible, James Bond, John Wick series)

**Score:** 22
**Body:** Hey there! Basically the title here. Ideally these characters will show tactical skills, resourcefulness and have an Achilles Heel or two. Thanks so much! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. *The Accountant* (2016)
2. The Man From Nowhere (2010)

Nobody (2021)

Man On Fire (2004)

The Transporter (2002)

Peppermint (2018)
3. Sisu
4. The bourne movies
5. The Equalizer trilogy
6. Jingle All the Way
7. Equilibrium- must see
8. Evil Dead has the ultimate badass
9. Taken

Nobody 

Collateral damage
10. Nobody

## 5. What are some unique movies?

**Score:** 18
**Body:** As the title says, I want some unique movies. Most of the movies that I have seen are a cheap copy of others with no creativity in them.

I have been searching for some movies with new concepts. I want to experience something that's not common in the movies like crime/murder mysteries, etc. However, if they have something great and new to offer then I am open to the suggestions.

Genre - ANY, Language - ANY

EDIT - I have watched "The Sixth Sense" , "Predestination", "Oldboy", LOtR trilogy, Memento, Parasite and others that I can't remember the names of right now. And I found these movies enjoyable and they were unique to my experience.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Filth and Wisdom (stars Eugene Hutz from the band Gogol Bordello; and, was directed by Madonna-something I didn't know when I first watched it.) 

Run Lola Run 

You mentioned watching some crime movies, I am sharing 'The Boondock Saints' is another amazing movie. ...The first one.
2. Being John Malkovich 

Donnie Darko 

Little Miss Sunshine 

Jojo Rabbit 

Mullholland Drive
3. Everything Everywhere All at Once

Also this Reddit post asks the same >
https://www.reddit.com/r/MovieSuggestions/s/zL60RCSfzs
4. Alphaville (1965)

Daisies (1966)

Playtime (1967)

Up! (1976)

Koyaanisqatsi (1982)

Tetsuo (1989)

Ghost Dog: The Way of the Samurai (1999)

Being John Malkovich (1999)

Corpus Callosum (2002)

9 Souls (2003)

Glory to the Filmmaker! (2007)

Final Cut: Ladies and Gentlemen (2012)
5. Amelie (2001)

The Adventures of Buckaroo Banzai Across the 8th Dimension (1984)

The Adventures of Baron Munchausen (1988)

Stardust (2007 - basically a zhuzhed up version of Hocus Pocus)
6. Parasite
7. Scott Pilgrim vs The World
8. Triplets of Belleville- a quirky animated French film, almost entirely without dialogue, about the Tour de France, a Mafia kidnapping, and old ladies starting a band. Plus, great soundtrack.
9. Wes Anderson, for a whimsical ride — ex. Grand Budapest Hotel
10. Dark City

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Which scene from a movie had you reacting like this?

**Score:** 806

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This scene from this movie.

It was DEVASTATING to his case.
  - Good call!
2. When Palpatine raises his arms and Star Destroyers materialize out of the dirt.
  - I was asking myself why I was being too much of a hater to watch that movie. Thanks for the reminder.
  - “Haha your lightsaber is no use against my sith lightning- OH SHIT TWO SABERS?!?”
3. Spectre, when they reveal that Blofeld is Bond’s brother and undermine the previous movies by staying it was all part of the plan.
4. Literally 95% of The Rise of Skywalker. There were so many retcons and plot conveniences that it almost felt like a parody
5. I was a teenager and went to an open air cinema in the town I was staying in for the summer. That night M:I2 was on screen. I still vividly remember that gun magically lifting in the air, and the full audience groaning in complete disappointment
6. In Prometheus when they arrive on an alien planet and just like that tra la la oh I know! Let's take off our helmets! That sounds clever. 
  - And the biologist trying to pet the aggressive and hostile mud snake cobra thing.

## 2. Is Back To Future that good??

**Score:** 534
**Body:** I really like the movie.. it’s a classic in my opinion, like a top 10 holidays film. BUT, hear me out.. is it really so good to get a 8.5 imdb. Just for comparison another classic Jurassic Park the original has a 8.2 imdb. It has a greater imdb score than freaking Dark Knight Rises, Revenant, The Wolf of Wall Street. I don’t think this score is justified.. thoughts??

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. They paid attention to every little detail, also the scenario is almost flawless.
  - Twin Pines/Lone Pine Mall was just delightful.
2. Yes
  - *Hell* yes.
    - That's heavy...
  - That's the power of love!
3. It was original, well done, and entertaining. Yes it was that good. Also when comparing to other films try thinking about it in terms of those made prior to its release, changes the perspective a bit.
4. so good they use the screenplay in film schools with some using it as the prefect screenplay. Everything has a purpose and is structured for its prefect story telling not a wasted scene.
  - The pacing on all three films is excellent.
5. If you have to try hard to find reasons to criticize it, then yes, it is

## 3. 3 hour movies that don't feel like 3 hour movies

**Score:** 364

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You're mistaken with Titanic.

Having to change the VHS tape was a clear sign that the film was going on a bit.
  - I usually only watched 1 of them. First tape if I wanted to see some boobs, second tape if I wanted to see some action.
    - I always joke that you can still find the VHS set at my parents' and I bet when you put it in, it starts right after the drawing scene ends, right where I left it. But that's probably spot on.
2. Titanic was on two VHS tapes dude cmon.
3. Oppenheimer was definitely 4-5 hours long.
  - It was only 5 hours?
4. Oppenheimer definitely doesn't feel like 3 hours long. It feels more like 6 hours.
5. It’s not 3 hours but OBAA is close to it and the movie feels like it’s only 90 minutes
6. Thin Red Line is two hours thirty minutes and feels like infinity because it transcends time and space. I can watch it on repeat forever
7. The Godfather.

## 4. Favourite Clancy Brown movie or tv show?

**Score:** 295

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Shawshank Redemption. 
2. Carnivàle (2003-05). Besides his performance, it has one of the best opening credit sequences I have seen.
  - This is the correct answer. God I wish the show could have gone on a few more seasons
3. Are you feeling it now,  Mr. Krabs?
4. He's had a lot of brilliant roles, but he'll always be The Kurgan to me.
  - it's better to burn out than to fade away
  - There can be only one
5. Starship Troopers. *The enemy can not push a button... if you disable his hand. Medic!*
6. It was honestly a decent role in my opinion but not a movie or TV show.
Detroit Become Human
7. Rawhide in Buckaroo Banzai

## 5. Remembering Corey Haim today, let us not forget the children who were exploited by industry and, when they had nothing else to give them, were left broken.

**Score:** 138

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Considering the amount of young stars that went through the Disney system.  One would think Disney could've done more.  Even now.  Britney Spears, Jake Lloyd, and on.
  - Jake Lloyd had nothing to do with Disney. The prequel series were made by Lucasfilm and 20th Century Fox in 1999. Disney didn't buy them for over a decade afterward.

He worked with 20th Century Fox, Miramax (Weinstein's company), Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer (now owned by Amazon), MTM Entertainment and Hallmark Entertainment.
  - Disney did so much more than you realize.

But it was mostly focused on facilitating and covering up the sexual abuse.
2. And let us also not forget Corey Feldman, who has had a lifetime of opportunity to name names and identify the predators, but refuses to because his "career" is more important than protecting children and bringing abusers to justice.
  - My understanding was he doesn’t name them as they are too powerful.
    - Too powerful, as in his life might be in danger? By naming them he's assured some level of security. He has also promised, multiple times, to call them out, but he always backpedals. He's more concerned about whatever little bit of fame he has.
      - You seem quick to judge someone who went through so much…
3. Where were/are the parents? Don’t excuse them. All kid actors need parent signatures to work. Don’t excuse them.
4. It wasn’t just the industry but, society itself. The media frequently played interviews of Corey Haim high on drugs (just as they played paparazzi clips of Anna Nicole Smith and Brittney Spears falling apart) for society’s entertainment. People joked about it, laughed at their misfortune/misery and had zero compassion.
5. Poor kid.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Critics like Paul Schrader,books or Internet accounts lead to them

**Score:** 23
**Body:** I enjoy both some of his old writings and his FB short review.

Though I don't agree with him all the times.I like his attitude and his taste for movies.What can go wrong with a man listing The Searchers as the greatest movie ever made? 

He not only seeks for movies theme and take a movie seriously,but he also have a sense for film cannon and discovering old,not so known important movies.

He got a Andrew Sarris American critic style,and this type of critism is gone.I tried to read the film comment but I cannot. BTW,anybody with ScriBD can help me？I want to read the article “Guilty Pleasure:Paul Schrader”

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Richard Brody, Jonathan Rosenbaum, perhaps … I'm not sure. Neither quite has Schrader's vibe, but they do have some similar touch points and preoccupations.  
  
If what you're really looking for is a contrarian, you could try out Armond White. He has by now degenerated into steady "anti-woke" pillorying of perfectly good films‚ but his history of reviews has a different and more interesting flavour.

Reading what 70s and 80s genre auteurs such as Walter Hill or John Milius had to say about technique might get you close to a Schrader feel. And as you probably know, Schrader's recent directorial output has been heavily Bergman-inspired.
  - I read some Rosenbaum but I am not very interested in him.

On a website I read some of John Milius short reviews,so crazy,I loved them.I think they are formally close to Schrader.
    - Yeah I mentioned Milius in part because he's an interesting guy, but also because I think he and Schrader share an overlapping US-flavoured right-libertarian fascination with fascism, warriors, Nietzsche etc. I think if you hunt a bit into whatever milieu they could be said to share from that period (eg Hill definitely influenced Milius) you'll find auteurish filmmakers and critics in the vein you're after. I'd go figure out which critics are obsessed with John Boorman's heyday, for instance.
      - Yeah,I understand what you said about their obesession about fascism and violence.I watched a not great exploitaion film Death Force the other day and I imagined Schrader can bring his understanding of Japanese fascism to those two Japanese soldier characters,and make the hero a true soul Emperor Army soldier,and with strong artistic ambition to flip on and satire the WW II narrative.

I don't know Hill influenced Milius and I think Hill just loves violent movies without having a big opinion about violence.

I‘d like to read the film comment for the Boorman critism,I remember Schrader wrote one but sadly I haven't seen Hell movie.
        - Beat Takeshi could be another one in the slot.

Edit: I just checked into any Hill and Milius relationship, and found this from Hill:

> "I wrote almost all of the Powers Boothe stuff, but I didn’t put in for credit. It was John Milius and one of his buddies. John and I have known each other for years. We were never close friends, but I liked him. Out of that whole generation of scriptwriters coming out in the 1970s, I thought John’s scripts were the best reads. They were like reading a good novel. I thought Paul Schrader wrote the deepest stuff though, the most intelligent. There was always a lot going on in Schrader’s scripts. I’m a great respecter of Paul and his work, the way he’s hung on. I thought I wrote the best tough-guy stuff, that was my little niche. But I think best screenwriter of our generation turned out to be Paul."

https://www.theguardian.com/film/2014/jul/17/walter-hill-action-movie-interview

One wonderful bit of film writing I'm aware of from Schrader is his "Notes on Film Noir", pretty easy to find online. It's very focused on technique and mood, and I reckon it says more about noir in its short length than the monographs I've looked at.
        - https://letterboxd.com/attentive/film/the-driver/

I'll add a plug for my own retrospective review of THE DRIVER as well.
2. I understand it might not quite be what you’re searching for but since Transcendental Style in Film is what I enjoy most of his and an important text in that mode of filmmaking, which is also my biggest jam, I can recommend ‘Slow Cinema’ edited by Tiago De Luca and Nuno Barrados Jorge. Maps out the concept through a collection of writings on topics related to it. I’ll also add ‘Directed by Yazujiro Ozu’ by Shiguehiko Hasumi which finally got an English translation not too long ago (a favorite director of Schrader he writes about in Transcendental Style in Film).
3. [is this the article?](https://s3.amazonaws.com/crawl.prod.proquest.com/fpcache/7a1f0fc4389112c5c2b8e54b0bc1031e.pdf?X-Amz-Security-Token=IQoJb3JpZ2luX2VjEKn%2F%2F%2F%2F%2F%2F%2F%2F%2F%2FwEaCXVzLWVhc3QtMSJHMEUCIBIaqGhT2w0lll2P1oEsdLjr6d2HSCjLSKCLKmEoi5wtAiEAner71ToNwx2j%2F5gBPSP7ZG%2B7oBhmufrWPaG0xYQfICwqlAIIcRAAGgw1MTgzNDY4NDgzNDEiDHa5Pp2LAPRlCL4cGirxAWldc%2BZUb52bSDd6mJobPCAsyL77eFgA0ENfPBzInDyQOvB9uJqQuXRafpSkh0CRBXuc1WK9nYH5nOp%2BPvbFtouLaBie2PFTlwRcmkbLZHnhuWWlJHlV1pfmakbcsYLxYccRrnmpvjyD1gFLdIhhdRU8f7sfsACCWivm8QU7SIIUwHK4pHBnCvByswBNCdCDdhecnxTaSlcYfRd4mDZDkjMBkMBwrW3Xybu%2BFZ7V8vJHJJwbJC6GdwveQWw85bxteUB%2F6ROi62Ozs7Ec3uydouoU0JYPAU8U742yWD0Q1myigx3XRR4vAT%2F788kT3dXMXCEw7uqmyAY6nQGrbCgi%2BhGEJpv6vYHwAO5fsu05B7s%2B%2ByeeMiR54hyZxzCuNfGTNXgSDwO6Yk5Nvxe92TcXaMjJl2V%2BwAHbxRJi4SxlwLTmZZmCz5tL6lbh%2FNyKw%2FdtPgVS%2BFmj3bdZb3GEeHU%2B3v%2B8g5udy0luVHAPEWEijVG9jtogzXqleV8tw2vta7tihV5U2Cgk0o%2Fb2yBSj4e31OJIYGKMZS35&X-Amz-Algorithm=AWS4-HMAC-SHA256&X-Amz-Date=20251104T082915Z&X-Amz-SignedHeaders=host&X-Amz-Credential=ASIAXRL7BHBK5IU4F2GQ%2F20251104%2Fus-east-1%2Fs3%2Faws4_request&X-Amz-Expires=3600&X-Amz-Signature=0a010c2e22532577d79b471ed3a4441a800e893aeaaab1c00fea11bcefc6f448#view=FitV)
  - I cannot access it
    - try this one -- https://www.proquest.com/openview/7a1f0fc4389112c5c2b8e54b0bc1031e/1?pq-origsite=gscholar&cbl=24820

its only the first page, but if its right, I'll look around for it

## 2. “Die My Love” Is Smaller Than Life

**Score:** 10
**Body:** As an exploration of the postpartum dangers to a woman’s mental health, “Die My Love” does the subject a disservice, Richard Brody writes. The movie, starring Jennifer Lawrence and Robert Pattinson, both sensationalizes those dangers and subordinates them to a general, social-existential vision of female frustrations and subjugations in marriage. For the record, the director Lynne Ramsay declared, soon after the “Die My Love” Cannes première, in May, that “this whole postpartum thing is just bullshit,” and added, “It’s about a relationship breaking down, it’s about love breaking down, and sex breaking down after having a baby. And it’s also about a creative block.” But directors are the authority on their intentions, not their results. “The movie proves to be about far more than she meant it to be—even if it does too little with all of its subjects,” Brody notes. Read his review of the film: [https://www.newyorker.com/culture/the-front-row/die-my-love-is-smaller-than-life](https://www.newyorker.com/culture/the-front-row/die-my-love-is-smaller-than-life)

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 3. The role of our expectations in enjoying a film

**Score:** 5
**Body:** I don’t think it would be controversial to say that most people prefer films that deliver exactly what they expect. That’s the reason why sequels and franchises reign supreme at the box office. Whenever a film deviates from its expected template, the reactions can be polarising. Recent examples such as 28 Years Later and Sinners would be examples of this. Both of them took a familiar genre and went in unexpected directions, and film-lovers have voiced strong opinions both for and against them. 

Professional critics tend to be more enthusiastic than mainstream audiences when films do the unexpected. It’s possibly because when your job is to watch more than one film a day, of all levels of quality, you tend to appreciate a break from the norm. Conversely the professionals are less keen whenever a film goes exactly where you’d expect it to go, such as the Avatar movies, even as audience enthusiasm causes them to break box office records.

I think appreciating the unexpected involves being open to the film and trusting it to take you where it wants you to go. It’s for people who are happy to have their assumptions questioned and subverted. But even when you enjoy the unexpected, it doesn’t always work. For instance A House of Dynamite subverts audience expectations with regard to where it’s going, and I don’t think it works at all. It feels like it’s promising something three times, but not delivering. And what it actually delivers instead isn’t very engaging.

Then there’s the unexpected in terms of tone. Taika Waititi’s films for example will flit between light and darkness in a way that clearly works for some, but not everyone. Edgar Wright’s Last Night in Soho somehow balances the angst of moving away from home with 1960s musical numbers and horror movie excess. I thought it was fantastic, but I get why many people didn’t. 

What are your thoughts regarding the unexpected? What makes something good-unexpected, and what makes it bad-unexpected? What makes a film a dizzying balancing act, and what makes it a confused mess?



### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think your instinct to want to conduct a broad survey of the habits of professional critics is a good one, and then also to want to test those impressions against the reactions of general audiences to various movies is interesting.

Logically, as you say, critics who see so many more films than a general audience member should be a bit more primed to welcome it when a script diverges from expectations, and reward such moves by filmmakers with praise. 

But I don’t know if this is necessarily exactly what’s at play in many cases. I would stress the fact that critics have their darlings, and that their evaluations can often be pretty shallow. Which came first, the fact that Alex Garland and Danny Boyle made a film which had unexpected swerves which were refreshing in the context of a legacy horror sequel, which garnered the critics’ approval — or the fact that a film which featured unexpected swerves for the generic context bore the names and reputations of these two filmmakers? (I’m just throwing this query out there, as I haven’t watched any Danny Boyle 28 Days movie at all.)

I’d just repeat, lots of filmmakers have huge cachet with critics, and I often don’t think it’s justified. Others then, for some reason, seem to have a black mark against them with critics, and this all seems to me to be extremely vibes-based rather than having to do more concretely with these respective directors’ films themselves. (To complicate this picture, I also firmly believe that critics enjoy to a great degree being fickle arbiters of the various auteurs’ reputations. “This year at Venice will I be a benevolent tyrant towards this name filmmaker, or will I send him into exile and recommend castigation?”)

By contrast to this, what you get from audience is the more authentic reception to a film.

In your post, you’re talking about all mainstream films and filmmakers. Those are the ones around which there comes to be a tangible public consensus about whether they’re good or bad. 

The critics also review lots of films which attain no footprint among the general audience or even generate enough online conversation in forums such as this one for one to get a clear feeling for how they were received by those who watched them. Suffice it to say, that there’s as much variability in quality among the smaller films as there is among the bigger mainstream ones. I think a lot of small films might get the darling treatment, like a rating of 75/100 even if the critic is actually wavering between a 50 and a 75.

Just for an example of what I mean by the darling treatment, I’ll cite this Metacritic pull-quote from a review of a film you mention in your post, Last Night in Soho. The rating accompanying this is 75/100:

>Last Night in Soho, with all its warts and wonders, shows you can teach an old dog some new tricks. Wright shows he still hasn't hit his ceiling as a filmmaker, but's heartening to see him stretch and reach rather than just keeping his artistic ambitions planted on the floor.
2. Part of it, most of it, is the art. No one knows anything and how a film will be received by audiences is always a huge question mark. 


I'd be curious to know if you're thinking only of modern movies or if if this in any way applies to older films. If it's just new movies then really you're asking about marketing and advertising more than the art of the filmmaking, at least to my mind.
  - Good questions! Although I am thinking of modern movies, I'm not intentionally excluding older ones. And I wasn't thinking of the expectations caused by marketing at all. That can of course cause people to have expectations that don't line up with what the movie actually is. 

I don't really watch trailers, so whenever my expectations were subverted regarding the above movies, it was due to my expectations regarding the genre, rather than the marketing, if that makes sense.
    - It does. 

That question made me think of Psycho, which was deliberately marketed and begins in a way that is meant to set the audience up for one thing, and then knocks them right to the ground. My mom saw it in the theater when it came out and she said she and the whole audience were all caught up in the question of whether Janet Leigh would get the money back before her boss found out she had taken it – and then she took a shower.
3. I definitely agree with this. I wonder if it's something to do with either being disappointed that it's not what to you expected, not necessarily bad, or rather, to do with the fact it is an unexpected thing, and that requires time to adjust to which viewers aren't able to do sitting there already in front of the film.
4. I think expectations play a smaller role than most people assume, and something going against expectations can also result in the pleasant surprise rather than the unpleasant one. However, I agree that with the layperson audience, there does seem to be something of a thread of disappointment when movies aren't what (they feel that) the trailer advertised.

Certainly though, I think critics tend to come down much more on the side of being in favor of the surprise. My favorite Ebert review, for example, is his review of *Joe vs. the Volcano*, in which he begins by expressing the joy of discovering a movie that is completely unexpected:

>Gradually during the opening scenes of “Joe Versus the Volcano,” my heart began to quicken, until finally I realized a wondrous thing: I had not seen this movie before. Most movies, I have seen before. Most movies, you have seen before. Most movies are constructed out of bits and pieces of other movies, like little engines built from cinematic Erector sets. But not “Joe Versus the Volcano.” It is not an entirely successful movie, but it is new and fresh and not shy of taking chances.
5. For me, the main thing I want from any great film is— show me something I haven’t seen before. Surprise me!

I have noticed that the movies that do that, that I love, that I recommend to friends and family, they tend to love too. And yet it’s coming as a recommendation, and I think that immediately changes expectations – my friends and family know what to expect from me. 

I’ve never understood the appeal of total predictability – which for me is indistinguishable from cliché – but there’s no question that:

what you hope for from a movie is absolutely going to how open/vulnerable you’re willing to be too what the film shows you.

(In terms of your other question – you can surprise me, but it needs to fit with what has come before, the characters need to remain consistent in some way— oh, and replacing one cliché with another is no joy. I’m thinking of It Stains the Sand Red, where they decided to throw a completely gratuitous graphic rape scene into the middle of a zombie movie – yeah, that was ‘unexpected’ for a whole bunch of *really good reasons,* because it shouldn’t have been there.)
6. >That’s the reason why sequels and franchises reign supreme at the box office.

I would argue that has a little more to do with the business side of filmmaking than anything else. From a business perspective, who are you more likely to give a loan to?

1. A guy with a good plan for a restaurant, but no experience or known presence.

2. A guy that wants to open another location of his already successful local restaurant, in hopes of having a local/regional chain over the course of the next decade?

Movies are all about money & that's why so many decisions occur that make no sense to us sometimes from a viewer perspective. "Why would they ever do that thing instead of this thing in that scene?" Somebody in charge thought one would either be cheaper or earn more than the other choice. At least most of the time. Sometimes, it's a money guy that thinks they're a creative & overrule the experts to prove it (insert giant mechanical spider story from Kevin Smith).

I think many of your other points are valid though for sure. It's also why sitcoms from 20+ years ago are the most popular things on streaming consistently, because life sucks right now & people find comfort in the familiar. That's not to say that having surprises isn't awesome sometimes, but Hollywood is risk adverse more & more as the MBAs run everything.

## 4. Mean Streets - Maybe Scorsese's best?

**Score:** 5
**Body:** After binging the new Scorsese docu series over the weekend, I felt compelled to revisit Mean Streets, as I've felt like I never gave it a proper shot. I first watched it as a Scorsese-obsessed teenager, but unlike Taxi Driver, Goodfellas, and Raging Bull, this one didn't do too much for me, in fact I only remember finding it quite boring. This time I was blown away. I found it absolutely brilliant. It's messy, it has some glaring technical problems, particularly with its subpar sound, it meanders quite a lot, some of the more peripheral performances can be a bit wonky, but weirdly none of that mattered in the end.

This thing is just so full of passion and overflowing with ideas, that I couldn't help but be charmed by it. One can feel a young Scorsese desperate to make a great film, with no patience to wait for the next film, so he's throwing it all in. This works quite nicely with the small scale of the film. It has so many cinematic ideas, yet the whole thing takes place within a couple of streets (duh) and between four or so characters. So you end up with a film that feels cinematically vast, yet intimate and personal. It really made me wish we had more small-scale Scorsese films like this.

A lot is said about Charlie's tension between Catholicism and the mob, which is quite compelling, but I found the true core of the film to be the tragic fraternal bond between Charlie and Johnny. This dynamic is incredibly potent, thanks to two clearly defined characters and two fantastic performances. Every scene Keitel and De Niro share, and I really wish there were more, is just pure dynamite. Those shots of them walking the streets together late at night and early in the morning are full of magic.

The film has a wonderful flow to it, a beautiful episodic nature where the scenes spill into each other in an almost dream-like way, but very subtly so. Often there is no obvious plot reason why we're seeing one scene after another, it instead follows an emotional and sensory logic. By the end of the film, it's like an orchestra the way it's all coming together. Scorsese's gift for montage was clearly already mastered at this point. I think this lack of plot and forward momentum is precisely why I found it so challenging when I was younger, it's probably the last thing I was expecting from a Scorsese mob movie, but it actually makes it such a unique work.

I was also surprised by how sensuous the film is. It has such a rich and textured visual identity. The red lights of the bar, the warmth of the sun coming in through the small apartment windows, the neon lights flooding the streets as they wander around, it all adds to such a surprisingly tender and warm experience. From the moment it opens on Harvey Keitel lying awake in bed, full of guilt, with the morning sun creeping in and the sound of traffic outside, there's immedietly so much life to it. I mean, those jump cuts as he lays back down? With the needle drop? Jesus Christ...

It has become an instant favorite for me, as you could probably tell. It almost feels like despite its status as a classic it has become a bit under-appreciated, but with a filmography like Scorsese's I guess it's to be expected.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don't think it's his best, but I do love it to pieces and I love how many of his hallmarks are there, most notably in the juxtaposition of faith and violence. The central performances are fantastic, and I love how slice-of-life it feels. There is a plot, but it's told in such a naturalistic way that it often feels like you're just watching this group of friends spend time together. It's excellent
  - Agreed. I thought it was remarkable how many of Scorcese’s trademarks were already on display: the focus on faith and guilt, the role of the mob, the voiceover narration, even the multiple Rolling Stones songs.
2. Goodfellas, Taxi Driver and The Departed are my faves but one thing Mean Streets does exceptionally well is look gritty, rough and artistically unpolished. 

My one critique of Scorsese (and truly a *minor* critique) is that sometimes his movies look too slick, polished and big budget. I applaud him for embracing CGI and going big but the gritty low budget feel of Mean Streets is something he doesn’t seem interested in recapturing. I kinda wish he had shot The Departed using a grainy filter or something.
3. * It almost feels like despite its status as a classic it has become a bit under-appreciated*

It’s so funny/interesting to see people project their experience with a film onto *everybody*.

“*I* didn’t fully appreciate this movie until today. 52 years later, I really appreciate it, therefore it’s a bit under appreciated in general.”

It’s not his best, but it’s appreciated for exactly what it is. One of history’s greatest filmmakers finding his voice. One of history’s great actors making an indelible first (big) impression.
  - Well I did say it has classic status, I’m aware of that. I’m just saying there’s like 10 Scorsese films that get brought up more frequently than Mean Streets, yet it’s absolutely top tier. Perhaps it’s a matter of it not translating as well to younger generations as some of his other classics.
4. Hard for me to say the best but I also LOVE LOVE LOVE IT! The way Harvey’s character has to choose between moving up and faith in his screw up friend. The Mob life is almost portrayed as the good choice. Like that’s what working hard is, to him. His cousin tho is what happens when it goes bad.
5. I've seen 28 films by Scorsese and it turns out I've seen Mean Streets more than any of this other films. The dynamic and rough-around-the-edges energy of the film is remarkable. When it was released in 1973 it was unique to American cinema for films released by companies like Warner Bros.
